description: Combined Design & Accessibility evaluation framework. Covers visual craft quality and WCAG 2.2 A/AA compliance as a single integrated audit. Deliberately excludes dimensions already owned by the CX audit (journey outcomes, task flow, trust signals, content quality, error recovery, navigation efficiency).
audit_scope: Visual design quality + WCAG 2.2 A/AA implementation — assessed from screenshots. Not: user journey outcomes, task completion, content strategy, or behavioural flows.
framework_sources: Google Material Design 3 — Visual & Accessibility guidelines
Apple Human Interface Guidelines — Visual design & accessibility
Baymard Institute — Form & Component UX benchmarks
Bajaj Finserv Design Principles (Design Creation, Design Application, Functionality Guidelines)

scoring_scale.9-10: Exemplary — fully realised, no observable issues. Meets or exceeds all relevant standards.
scoring_scale.7-8: Strong — well-executed with only minor gaps. Follows best practices with isolated exceptions.
scoring_scale.5-6: Acceptable — minimum standards mostly met but notable gaps visible. Needs improvement.
scoring_scale.3-4: Weak — significant problems that degrade the experience or violate standards. Needs priority fixes.
scoring_scale.0-2: Critical failure — violates the core intent of this dimension. Causes user harm, task failure, or a clear WCAG A/AA breach.

issue_severity.critical: Causes task failure, data loss, or is a WCAG Level A violation. Must fix before launch.
issue_severity.high: WCAG Level AA violation or major visual quality failure. Fix in next sprint.
issue_severity.medium: Noticeable deviation from standards or Bajaj principles without being a WCAG breach. Fix near-term.

dimensions.name: Typography System Execution
dimensions.definition: Evaluate the consistency, readability, scalability, accessibility, and visual effectiveness of typography across the interface — covering font hierarchy, heading/body differentiation, font scaling, line height, spacing, and legibility across sections and devices.
dimensions.what_this_is_not: Not about whether content is readable to the user as a journey outcome (that is CX). This is about whether the designer correctly executed the type system at pixel level.
standards: WCAG 2.2 SC 1.4.4 — Resize Text (AA): text must scale to 200% without loss of content
WCAG 2.2 SC 1.4.12 — Text Spacing (AA): no content loss when line-height ≥1.5x, letter-spacing ≥0.12em
Bajaj: Crisp & Clear fonts — defined size, weight, line height per content level
Bajaj: separate height defined for header/subtitle vs body
Material Design: minimum 12sp caption, 14sp body, 16sp+ primary body
screenshot_checks: Are there at least 3 visually distinct typographic levels (heading → body → caption)?
Is body text at least 14px? Is it meaningfully larger than captions or footnotes?
Is line height generous — text lines not cramped together?
Are font weights used with intention — bold for headings, regular for body, not all one weight?
Is a single font family applied consistently — or are multiple families mixed without purpose?
Are financial figures (amounts, rates, EMI) in a larger or heavier style than surrounding text?
Is typography rhythm balanced — consistent scale across sections?
Does heading/body differentiation remain clear across all visible pages?

scoring_rubric.9-10: Excellent typography execution: 3+ clear typographic levels, consistent font usage, comfortable readability, proper spacing and line height, strong visual rhythm, accessible text presentation.
scoring_rubric.7-8: Strong typography system with minor inconsistencies — e.g. subheadings and body text too similar in size, or line height tight in one section.
scoring_rubric.5-6: Moderate typography issues affecting readability and cohesion — only 2 visible levels, or most text at similar size/weight.
scoring_rubric.3-4: Weak typography hierarchy causing usability friction — no meaningful hierarchy, overcrowded text blocks, misaligned typography.
scoring_rubric.0-2: Severely poor typography significantly harming readability and accessibility — all text same weight/size, dense unreadable blocks.

common_violations: Disclaimer or T&C text indistinguishably small versus body text
All text rendered in the same font weight with no emphasis
Line height so tight that text lines appear to merge
Interest rate or EMI figure presented in the same size as surrounding body copy
Multiple font families mixed with no design rationale
Excessive typography variations with no consistent scale

dimensions.name: Color System Application & Contrast
dimensions.definition: Evaluate the effectiveness, consistency, accessibility, and usability of color usage across the interface — covering WCAG contrast compliance, functional color usage, CTA visibility, state color differentiation, brand color consistency, and text/background readability.
dimensions.what_this_is_not: Not about whether colour makes users trust the brand (that is CX). This is about pixel-level contrast measurement and palette correctness.
standards: WCAG 2.2 SC 1.4.3 — Contrast Minimum (AA): 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text (18pt or 14pt bold)
WCAG 2.2 SC 1.4.11 — Non-text Contrast (AA): 3:1 for UI components (input borders, focus rings, icon outlines)
WCAG 2.2 SC 1.4.1 — Use of Colour (A): colour must not be the sole means of conveying information
Bajaj: brand colour palette applied correctly — no off-brand colours introduced without purpose
Bajaj: semantic colours correct — green for success, red for error, amber for warning
screenshot_checks: Does body text appear clearly readable against its background — not light grey on white?
Are CTA buttons visually distinct from the page background with strong contrast?
Are form field borders visible against the page background (not invisible on white)?
Are error, success, and warning states accompanied by icon or text — not colour alone?
Are disabled elements visually distinct from enabled ones beyond just colour?
Are link colours distinguishable from surrounding body text?
Is brand color applied consistently across all visible sections?

scoring_rubric.9-10: Excellent color implementation: accessible contrast ratios, consistent color language, clear CTA emphasis, effective state communication, strong visual differentiation.
scoring_rubric.7-8: Strong color system with minor contrast or consistency issues — e.g. secondary text slightly light, or one form border barely visible against white.
scoring_rubric.5-6: Moderate color inconsistencies affecting usability — noticeable contrast issues on secondary content, or one semantic state relies solely on colour.
scoring_rubric.3-4: Weak contrast and poor visual communication — visually obvious failures, light grey text on white, nearly invisible form borders.
scoring_rubric.0-2: Severe accessibility and readability failures caused by poor color implementation — multiple WCAG 1.4.3 violations, error states marked only by colour.

common_violations: Light grey placeholder or helper text nearly indistinguishable from the background
CTA button text at borderline contrast against button background colour
Form input border invisible against white page background
Error state shown only as a red border — no error icon or text
Off-brand accent colour introduced in a promotional section
Ambiguous state colors with no supporting text or icon differentiation

dimensions.name: Layout Grid & Spatial Rhythm
dimensions.definition: Evaluate layout structure, spacing consistency, alignment quality, and spatial organization — covering grid consistency, alignment precision, spacing rhythm, white space usage, layout organization, section grouping, and structural balance.
dimensions.what_this_is_not: Not about whether users can navigate or find content (that is CX). This is about whether the layout grid is correctly executed.
standards: WCAG 2.2 SC 1.4.10 — Reflow (AA): content must not require horizontal scrolling at 320px viewport width
WCAG 2.2 SC 1.3.4 — Orientation (AA): content not restricted to one screen orientation
Bajaj: clear compartments — distinguishable separators, defined gutter space between components
Bajaj: margin & spacing defined in library — consistent application expected
Material Design: 8dp base grid — all spacing values multiples of 8 (or 4 for fine-grained)
screenshot_checks: Is there consistent left/right margin — content not running edge-to-edge?
Are sections clearly separated — not bleeding into each other?
Are cards and containers consistently padded internally?
Do similar components (two cards, two form fields) have the same spacing between them?
Is whitespace used intentionally as a separator, or does the layout feel uniformly dense?
Does content appear to fit within the viewport — no obvious horizontal overflow?
Is spatial organization predictable and balanced across sections?

scoring_rubric.9-10: Highly polished layouts: consistent margins and gutters, effective whitespace usage, predictable structure, clean spatial organization, balanced layouts.
scoring_rubric.7-8: Strong layout organization with minor spacing inconsistencies — isolated deviations, one section with tighter padding, or card spacing slightly varies.
scoring_rubric.5-6: Moderate layout weaknesses affecting cohesion — spacing inconsistent, some sections cramped, margins vary across pages.
scoring_rubric.3-4: Weak structural organization causing confusion — no apparent grid, elements misaligned, sections bleed into each other.
scoring_rubric.0-2: Severely disorganized layouts significantly harming usability — content runs edge-to-edge, chaotic visual structure, poor grouping.

common_violations: Text or cards running flush to screen edge with zero margin
Two cards of the same type with different internal padding
Sections stacked with no visible gutter — they blur into each other
One page section with tight padding while adjacent sections are generous
Content visible overflowing horizontally on a mobile viewport
Poor section grouping making structure unclear

dimensions.name: Visual Hierarchy & Attention Guidance
dimensions.definition: Evaluate how effectively the design guides user attention toward important content and actions — covering CTA prominence, visual prioritization, reading flow, emphasis hierarchy, attention distribution, primary vs secondary action clarity, and scanning patterns.
dimensions.what_this_is_not: Not about whether users can find information in a journey (that is CX 'Visual Hierarchy and Scannability'). This is about whether the size/weight/contrast system is correctly designed to guide attention.
standards: WCAG 2.2 SC 1.3.1 — Info and Relationships (A): visual structure should encode information hierarchy
Gestalt principles: figure-ground, similarity, proximity — applied to information architecture
Bajaj: primary actions and objectives within the first 2 folds
Bajaj: reduce cognitive load — visual weight directs attention to what matters
screenshot_checks: Can you identify the single most visually dominant element in under 3 seconds?
Are there at least 3 distinct visual weight levels — primary (largest/boldest), secondary, tertiary?
Do secondary elements clearly recede — smaller, lighter, or lower contrast than primary?
Is the primary CTA visually heavier than secondary actions on the same screen?
Are key financial figures (rate, EMI, tenure) visually elevated above surrounding text?
Is promotional/decorative content visually subordinate to functional content?
Are focal points clear or do competing elements create attention confusion?

scoring_rubric.9-10: Excellent attention guidance: clear focal points, strong CTA visibility, effective prioritization, logical reading flow, clear visual grouping — eye immediately lands on most important element.
scoring_rubric.7-8: Strong hierarchy with minor prioritization issues — e.g. primary and secondary CTAs at the same visual weight, or financial figures in body text style.
scoring_rubric.5-6: Moderate hierarchy weaknesses affecting scanability — multiple elements compete at same level, or the most important element is not the most visually prominent.
scoring_rubric.3-4: Weak hierarchy causing confusion and friction — competing focal points, weak CTA emphasis, poor prioritization.
scoring_rubric.0-2: Severely ineffective hierarchy significantly harming usability — flat design, everything same weight, no dominant element, page intent unclear.

common_violations: Two buttons on the same screen with identical visual treatment (filled + filled)
EMI or interest rate figure in normal body text weight alongside product description
Hero banner image dominating the viewport while the primary CTA is small and low-contrast
Section headings barely larger than body text
Promotional tag or badge visually larger than the product's key financial metric
Cluttered attention flow with no clear reading path

dimensions.name: Component Design Quality & System Consistency
dimensions.definition: Evaluate the consistency, scalability, and visual reliability of reusable UI components and patterns — covering component consistency, button styling, card structures, input styling, reusable pattern consistency, design system adherence, state consistency, and predictability of components.
dimensions.what_this_is_not: Not about whether components function correctly in a flow (that is CX). This is about whether each component is visually well-crafted and systemically consistent.
standards: Bajaj: re-use & re-purpose — components from the design library, not one-off bespoke designs
Bajaj: flat learning curve — widely accepted component patterns applied correctly
Material Design 3: component proportions — button height 40dp, input height 56dp, card elevation
Apple HIG: consistent corner radius, weight, and shadow across component types
screenshot_checks: Do primary buttons have a filled background with sufficient internal padding (not cramped text)?
Do cards use a consistent corner radius and elevation across the page?
Do input fields appear tall enough to tap easily — not hairline-thin?
Are chips, tags, and badges proportional — not oversized or too small relative to their label?
Do components look complete — no rough edges, unbalanced padding, or misaligned internal elements?
Is the visual finish consistent — not some components looking polished and others looking draft-quality?
Are interaction patterns consistent and predictable across all component instances?

scoring_rubric.9-10: Excellent component consistency: unified component system, consistent interaction patterns, scalable reusable components, predictable behaviors, strong visual cohesion — mature design system quality.
scoring_rubric.7-8: Strong component quality with minor inconsistencies — one component type appears rough, e.g. input fields too thin or card corner radii inconsistent.
scoring_rubric.5-6: Moderate inconsistencies affecting cohesion — component quality uneven, some polished, some clearly draft, noticeable proportion or padding issues.
scoring_rubric.3-4: Weak component quality causing fragmentation — multiple components appear unfinished, conflicting interaction patterns, weak design system cohesion.
scoring_rubric.0-2: Severely inconsistent component system significantly harming usability — no system discipline, visual fragmentation, unpredictable component behavior.

common_violations: CTA button with text almost touching the button border (insufficient horizontal padding)
Cards with different border-radius values on the same screen (e.g. 4px and 12px)
Input field so thin it appears unclickable (less than 36px height on mobile)
Badge or chip with text overflowing its container
Bottom sheet with misaligned handle indicator or inconsistent corner radius
Visual fragmentation between component types breaking design system cohesion

dimensions.name: Interactive States & Focus Design
dimensions.definition: Evaluate the visibility, accessibility, and usability of interaction states and focus behaviors — covering hover states, active states, focus visibility, disabled states, keyboard accessibility, state differentiation, and interaction feedback.
dimensions.what_this_is_not: Not about whether users can navigate or control flows (that is CX 'User Control and Flexibility'). This is about whether states are visually designed at component level.
standards: WCAG 2.2 SC 2.4.7 — Focus Visible (AA): keyboard focus indicator must be visible
WCAG 2.2 SC 2.4.11 — Focus Appearance (AA): focus indicator must have 3:1 contrast and enclose the component
WCAG 2.2 SC 1.4.1 — Use of Colour (A): disabled state must not rely on colour alone
Bajaj: clear action callouts — interactive elements visually distinct in all states
screenshot_checks: Are disabled form fields or buttons visually different from their enabled state — reduced opacity or muted colour?
If any element shows a focus state in the screenshot, is the focus ring clearly visible (not a faint dotted line)?
Do selected tabs, active steps, or chosen options show a clear visual active state?
Are pressed/active states on buttons designed — or do buttons look identical before and after tap?
Are toggle switches, checkboxes, and radio buttons clearly designed in both checked and unchecked states?
Is keyboard accessibility visible through clearly designed focus indicators?

scoring_rubric.9-10: Excellent interaction state visibility: clear focus indicators, distinct interaction states, accessible keyboard navigation, predictable interaction behavior, strong feedback visibility.
scoring_rubric.7-8: Strong interaction design with minor issues — most states designed but one is weak, e.g. disabled state only slightly muted or selected tab only indicated by colour.
scoring_rubric.5-6: Moderate state inconsistencies affecting usability — some interactive elements have states, others appear identical in all states.
scoring_rubric.3-4: Weak state visibility causing interaction confusion — disabled looks same as enabled, active tab indistinguishable from inactive.
scoring_rubric.0-2: Severely inaccessible or unclear interaction states — states largely absent, invisible focus indicators, no keyboard accessibility support.

common_violations: Disabled button identical in appearance to the enabled button except for being slightly greyed
Active navigation tab differentiated only by a barely visible colour shift
Radio button selected state indistinguishable from unselected at a glance
Step indicator in a multi-step form not visually showing the current step vs completed steps
Toggle switch with no visible on/off label and only colour to differentiate states
Missing or invisible keyboard focus indicators

dimensions.name: Iconography & Visual Communication Quality
dimensions.definition: Evaluate the clarity, consistency, recognizability, and effectiveness of icons and visual communication elements — covering icon consistency, semantic clarity, visual recognizability, symbol interpretation, supporting visual cues, and style consistency.
dimensions.what_this_is_not: Not about whether users understand navigation (that is CX). This is about the visual quality and accessibility of the icon set itself.
standards: WCAG 2.2 SC 1.1.1 — Non-text Content (A): icons that convey information must have a text alternative
WCAG 2.2 SC 1.3.3 — Sensory Characteristics (A): instructions must not rely solely on shape, icon, or visual position
Bajaj: icons over images — graphical symbols preferred for functionality over photographic images
Bajaj: icons must be self-explanatory or accompanied by text labels
Material Design: icon size system — 16/20/24/32dp; consistent weight (outlined or filled, not mixed)
screenshot_checks: Are all standalone navigation or action icons accompanied by a visible text label?
Are icons from a consistent visual set — all line-weight, all filled, or all outlined — not mixed?
Do icons appear at a consistent size (e.g. all navigation icons 24px, all inline icons 16px)?
Are functional icons (menu, search, back, close) clearly recognisable — not ambiguous custom illustrations?
Are product/feature icons accompanied by text — no icon-only product navigation on mobile?
Are icons used where they add clarity, or are they purely decorative?

scoring_rubric.9-10: Excellent icon clarity: clear icon meanings, consistent visual style, intuitive communication, strong recognizability, helpful supporting visuals — all standalone icons labelled.
scoring_rubric.7-8: Strong iconography with minor clarity issues — one icon type breaks the style, or 1–2 standalone icons without labels.
scoring_rubric.5-6: Moderate communication inconsistencies — inconsistent icon style across the page, or multiple standalone navigation icons without labels.
scoring_rubric.3-4: Weak icon clarity affecting usability — ambiguous icons, inconsistent icon styles, confusing symbolism.
scoring_rubric.0-2: Severely confusing or inconsistent visual communication — icon-only navigation with no labels, mixed styles and sizes, icons replacing text where text is clearer.

common_violations: Bottom navigation bar with icons and no text labels
Mix of outlined and filled icons on the same screen
Product category navigation using icon-only tiles on mobile
Custom illustration used as an action icon — ambiguous meaning
Icons at different sizes (16px and 32px) within the same navigation row
Decorative icon overload adding visual noise without aiding comprehension

dimensions.name: Touch Ergonomics & Spatial Accessibility
dimensions.definition: Evaluate touch usability, spatial accessibility, and ergonomic quality of touch interactions — covering touch target sizing, touch spacing, reachability, tap accuracy, mobile ergonomics, gesture accessibility, and interaction comfort.
dimensions.what_this_is_not: Not about whether mobile experience supports user journeys (that is CX 'Mobile and Touch Experience'). This is about the pixel-level sizing implementation of interactive elements.
standards: WCAG 2.2 SC 2.5.8 — Target Size Minimum (AA): 24x24 CSS px minimum; if smaller, 24px spacing required around it
WCAG 2.2 SC 2.5.5 — Target Size Enhanced (AAA): 44x44 CSS px recommended
Bajaj Functionality Guidelines: minimum 40x40px tap target for close/back/navigation icons
Bajaj: tapability & reachability — sufficient spacing around tappable elements to prevent accidental taps
Google Material Design: 48dp minimum touch target recommendation
screenshot_checks: Do primary CTA buttons appear at least 44px tall — visually generous, not hairline?
Are small icons (close ✕, info ⓘ, filter, share) appearing to have an extended tap area or sufficient visual size?
Are adjacent tappable elements (two buttons, radio options, list items) spaced apart — not directly touching?
Are form field input areas visually tall enough to tap without precision (aim for 44px+)?
Are text-only links used as the sole action in areas where a button would provide a larger target?
Are tappable list items in menus or drawers given enough vertical height to tap accurately?
Are interactive areas comfortable to reach with a thumb on a standard mobile viewport?

scoring_rubric.9-10: Excellent touch ergonomics: comfortable touch interactions, proper touch spacing, accessible touch targets, mobile-friendly ergonomics, reduced accidental interactions.
scoring_rubric.7-8: Strong touch usability with minor issues — primary CTAs adequate but some secondary interactive elements appear small.
scoring_rubric.5-6: Moderate touch usability concerns — multiple small interactive elements visible, critical targets immediately adjacent.
scoring_rubric.3-4: Weak ergonomics causing usability friction — small touch targets, crowded interactive areas, poor reachability, accidental tap risk.
scoring_rubric.0-2: Severely inaccessible touch interactions — critical interactive elements clearly undersized, close button appears tiny with no padding.

common_violations: Close (✕) icon rendered at 16px with no surrounding tap zone
Two adjacent filter icons with less than 8px spacing between tap areas
Form input field less than 36px tall making tap inaccurate
Text-only 'Change' or 'Edit' links with no button treatment in a critical flow
Radio button options stacked with less than 4px vertical gap between rows
Weak touch accessibility for users with motor impairments

dimensions.name: Form Design & Label Accessibility
dimensions.definition: Evaluate the usability, accessibility, clarity, and completion efficiency of forms and input systems — covering label clarity, input readability, validation visibility, form grouping, required field communication, accessibility support, and completion ease.
dimensions.what_this_is_not: Not about whether forms are easy to complete in a journey (that is CX 'Form Design and Data Collection UX'). This is about the visual and WCAG implementation of form components.
standards: WCAG 2.2 SC 1.3.1 — Info and Relationships (A): labels must be programmatically associated (persistent visible label is the observable proxy)
WCAG 2.2 SC 3.3.1 — Error Identification (A): errors must be described in text, not only visually
WCAG 2.2 SC 3.3.2 — Labels or Instructions (A): every input must have a visible label
WCAG 2.2 SC 3.3.4 — Error Prevention (AA): for financial data entry, reversibility or review step required
Bajaj: relatable structuring — logically grouped fields; all demographic fields clustered together
screenshot_checks: Does every visible input field have a persistent text label above or beside it — not placeholder text alone?
Are required fields clearly marked — asterisk, 'Required' label, or equivalent — not colour alone?
Are related fields visually grouped (e.g. personal details in one card, financial details in another)?
If an error state is visible — does it show a specific error message in text below the field, not just a red border?
Are sensitive fields (PAN, Aadhaar, bank details) accompanied by an info icon or trust note?
Are form fields visually single-column on mobile — not side-by-side in a two-column layout?
Is completion flow smooth with logical field sequencing and clear navigation?

scoring_rubric.9-10: Excellent form usability and accessibility: clear labels, logical grouping, helpful validation, accessible input design, smooth completion flow — all fields have persistent visible labels.
scoring_rubric.7-8: Strong form experience with minor issues — one field uses placeholder-only labelling, or error state shows red border without text description.
scoring_rubric.5-6: Moderate usability concerns affecting completion — 2+ fields use placeholder-only labels, error marking relies on colour alone.
scoring_rubric.3-4: Weak form structure causing friction — missing labels, confusing forms, weak validation messaging, accessibility barriers.
scoring_rubric.0-2: Severely unusable or inaccessible forms — placeholder-only labels throughout, no error text descriptions, chaotic field order.

common_violations: OTP input field with placeholder '• • • •' and no visible label
PAN number field with only placeholder text 'Enter PAN' — label disappears on focus
Required field marked only by making the border red when empty — no asterisk or label
Error state shown as red border only — no text explaining what is wrong or how to fix it
Mobile form with two fields side-by-side forcing horizontal eye movement
Ambiguous input fields with unclear expected format or content

dimensions.name: System Feedback & Status Visibility
dimensions.definition: Evaluate how effectively the interface communicates status, progress, and system responsiveness — covering loading indicators, progress visibility, confirmation states, error/success messaging, wait-state communication, and responsiveness feedback.
dimensions.what_this_is_not: Not about whether the system gives users enough feedback in a journey (that is CX 'System Feedback and Load Experience'). This is about whether the visual design of feedback components meets standards.
standards: WCAG 2.2 SC 4.1.3 — Status Messages (AA): status messages must be perceivable without receiving focus
WCAG 2.2 SC 1.4.1 — Use of Colour (A): status must not be conveyed by colour alone
Bajaj: relevant assistance — in-context feedback related to the user's current state
Bajaj: avoid dead ends — journey-end screens always show a next action
Material Design: loading states (skeleton, circular progress), toast/snackbar, inline error design
screenshot_checks: If a loading state is visible — is it a spinner, skeleton, or progress bar (not a blank white screen)?
If a success state is visible — does it use a green icon + text confirmation (not green colour alone)?
If an error state is visible — does it use a red icon + specific error text (not red colour alone)?
Are toast notifications visually styled distinctly — not just plain text appearing on the page?
Is the empty state screen (no results, no data) designed with an illustration or icon + message?
Are status banners positioned where they will be immediately noticed?
Is system responsiveness clearly communicated to avoid user uncertainty?

scoring_rubric.9-10: Excellent feedback visibility: clear loading communication, transparent progress indicators, timely confirmations, predictable system feedback — all visible feedback states use icon + text.
scoring_rubric.7-8: Strong status communication with minor gaps — most states designed but one relies on colour alone, or one status message positioned poorly.
scoring_rubric.5-6: Moderate inconsistencies affecting clarity — success or error states designed but one is colour-only, loading states may be blank.
scoring_rubric.3-4: Weak feedback visibility causing uncertainty — missing feedback, invisible loading states, delayed communication, ambiguous status messaging.
scoring_rubric.0-2: Severely inadequate or missing feedback systems — multiple feedback states rely solely on colour, loading states are blank screens.

common_violations: Success confirmation shown as green text only — no icon or visual emphasis
Error state on a form shown only as a red border with no icon or text
Loading screen is a blank white page with no indicator
Empty search results shown as a blank list — no illustration, message, or suggestion
Status banner appearing at the very bottom of a long screen below the fold
Weak responsiveness indicators leaving users uncertain about system state

dimensions.name: Brand Identity & Visual Consistency
dimensions.definition: Evaluate the cohesiveness, recognizability, and consistency of visual branding across the interface — covering brand styling consistency, visual identity cohesion, asset consistency, tone alignment, aesthetic consistency, and brand recognizability.
dimensions.what_this_is_not: Not about whether users trust Bajaj as a brand (that is CX 'Trust and Credibility'). This is about visual design coherence across screens.
standards: Bajaj: re-use & re-purpose — consistent use of design library components
Bajaj: crisp & clear fonts — brand typography applied uniformly
Bajaj: design creation principle — usability over marketing, no dark-pattern visual tricks
ISO 9241-110 — Conformity with user expectations: consistent appearance across the product
Cognitive accessibility: consistent design language reduces the mental effort required to learn new screens
screenshot_checks: Does every screen feel like it belongs to the same product — consistent colour, type, and component style?
Are the brand's primary colours used correctly across all screens (not replaced by off-brand alternatives)?
Is the header/navigation bar visually identical across all screens?
Is the photography or illustration style consistent — not mixing stock photography and custom illustration?
Are promotional screens using the same design language as functional screens?
Does the overall aesthetic feel professionally unified or like multiple designers worked without coordination?

scoring_rubric.9-10: Excellent brand cohesion: strong brand cohesion, unified aesthetics, recognizable visual identity, consistent design language — all screens visually unified.
scoring_rubric.7-8: Strong branding with minor inconsistencies — one screen or section breaks the visual language, e.g. a promotional banner uses different font style.
scoring_rubric.5-6: Moderate visual identity inconsistencies — different button treatments, inconsistent header styles, or photography mixed with flat illustration.
scoring_rubric.3-4: Weak cohesion affecting perception — screens look inconsistent, brand colours inconsistently applied, fragmented branding.
scoring_rubric.0-2: Severely fragmented or inconsistent branding — screens look like different products, no visual thread connecting them.

common_violations: Promotional landing page using a completely different colour palette from the product pages
Navigation header restyled on one specific flow (different background colour or height)
Stock photography used on some screens and branded illustrations on others with no visual transition
Brand primary colour replaced by a similar but off-brand blue on one section
One screen using a rounded card style while adjacent screens use sharp-cornered cards
Mismatched visual assets breaking identity cohesion

dimensions.name: Cognitive Accessibility & Information Density
dimensions.definition: Evaluate whether information and visual presentation remain cognitively manageable and accessible — covering cognitive load, information density, content chunking, simplicity, mental processing burden, visual overload, and comprehension effort.
dimensions.what_this_is_not: Not about whether users can complete tasks without being overwhelmed (that is CX 'Cognitive Load and Decision Simplicity'). This is about whether the visual design implements cognitive accessibility techniques correctly.
standards: WCAG 2.2 SC 1.4.10 — Reflow: single-column layout on mobile to reduce spatial complexity
WCAG 2.1 — Cognitive Accessibility Guidance: chunk information, use visual grouping, limit choice
Bajaj: reduce cognitive load — limit fields and choices shown at any stage
Bajaj: one action at a time — no page to have more than one Primary CTA
Bajaj: relatable structuring — familiar patterns that reduce mental effort
Miller's Law: 7±2 items in working memory — apply to visible options and fields
screenshot_checks: Is a single dominant task or decision point visible per screen — or are multiple tasks competing?
Are there more than 6 visible form fields simultaneously on a mobile screen?
Is detailed or secondary information hidden behind accordions, info icons, or 'Read more' — or all expanded at once?
Are key financial figures (rate, EMI, tenure) visually extracted and highlighted — not buried in dense paragraphs?
Does the screen feel visually overwhelming — too many options, sections, cards, or CTAs at equal weight?
Are icons used to reduce reading burden — or is everything expressed only in dense text blocks?

scoring_rubric.9-10: Exceptionally clear and cognitively accessible presentation: digestible information structure, reduced cognitive strain, clear chunking, simple presentation patterns, balanced visual density.
scoring_rubric.7-8: Strong cognitive accessibility with minor overload risks — one screen presents too many options simultaneously, or important detail not extracted from dense text.
scoring_rubric.5-6: Moderate complexity affecting comprehension — multiple competing tasks on some screens, or 6+ form fields shown at once without grouping.
scoring_rubric.3-4: High cognitive burden causing usability issues — overwhelming information density, excessive complexity, cluttered presentation, poor content prioritization.
scoring_rubric.0-2: Severely overwhelming or cognitively inaccessible presentation — all content expanded simultaneously, key figures buried, no progressive disclosure, no visual breathing room.

common_violations: EMI calculator, product benefits, eligibility criteria, and a CTA all competing equally on one mobile screen
Interest rate buried inside a 4-line product description paragraph with no visual extraction
8+ form fields visible simultaneously on a single mobile screen with no section grouping
All accordions expanded by default, presenting a wall of text on the initial page load
Promotional banner, product CTA, and cross-sell CTA all at the same visual weight on one screen
Overwhelming visual density with no progressive disclosure or content chunking
